item: paper_b>BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding

markdown:
# Original Transformer vs BERT: A Three-Dimensional Comparison

## 1. Architecture Role

| Dimension | Original Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) | BERT (Devlin et al., 2018) |
|---|---|---|
| **Component used** | Full encoder–decoder | Encoder stack only |
| **Purpose** | General sequence transduction model | Representation model for language understanding |

The original Transformer is a "new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely" introduced as a general sequence-to-sequence model [1]. Its "overall architecture using stacked self-attention and point-wise, fully connected layers for both the encoder and decoder" with each side comprising N = 6 identical layers [2].

BERT, by contrast, "is a multi-layer bidirectional Transformer encoder based on the original implementation described in Vaswani et al. (2017)" [3]. It uses no decoder at all — the encoder-only design is what enables BERT to serve as a universal feature extractor for downstream NLP tasks.

## 2. Directionality

The two models take fundamentally different approaches to the direction of information flow.

**Transformer — mixed directionality.** The encoder uses unrestricted self-attention ("each position in the encoder can attend to all positions in the previous layer of the encoder" [4]), but the decoder is left-to-right only:

> "We also modify the self-attention sub-layer in the decoder stack to prevent positions from attending to subsequent positions. This masking, combined with fact that the output embeddings are offset by one position, ensures that the predictions for position i can depend only on the known outputs at positions less than i." [2]

> "We need to prevent leftward information flow in the decoder to preserve the auto-regressive property. We implement this inside of scaled dot-product attention by masking out (setting to −∞) all values in the input of the softmax which correspond to illegal connections." [4]

**BERT — fully bidirectional.** Every token can attend to every other token in every layer:

> "Critically, however, the BERT Transformer uses bidirectional self-attention, while the GPT Transformer uses constrained self-attention where every token can only attend to context to its left." [3]

> "the MLM objective enables the representation to fuse the left and the right context, which allows us to pre-train a deep bidirectional Transformer." [5]

The ablation study in BERT further quantifies the cost of dropping bidirectionality: "The LTR model performs worse than the MLM model on all tasks, with large drops on MRPC and SQuAD" [6].

## 3. Training Objective

| Dimension | Original Transformer | BERT |
|---|---|---|
| **Paradigm** | Supervised sequence-to-sequence | Unsupervised pre-training + supervised fine-tuning |
| **Loss** | Cross-entropy with label smoothing on next-token prediction (translation) | Cross-entropy on masked-token prediction + binary next-sentence classification |
| **Pre-training task** | N/A — directly trained on WMT translation | Masked LM (MLM) + Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) |
| **Data** | WMT 2014 EN–DE / EN–FR parallel corpora | Unlabeled monolingual text |

**Transformer** is trained end-to-end on machine translation. The training section uses "label smoothing of value ϵ_ls = 0.1" [7], optimizing cross-entropy next-token prediction at each decoder position on the WMT 2014 English-to-German and English-to-French datasets [7].

**BERT** is pre-trained on two self-supervised tasks simultaneously [8]:

- **Task #1 — Masked LM (MLM):** 15% of WordPiece tokens are chosen for prediction; of those, 80% are replaced with [MASK], 10% with a random token, and 10% left unchanged. The model predicts the original token with cross-entropy loss. "In contrast to denoising auto-encoders, we only predict the masked words rather than reconstructing the entire input." [8]
- **Task #2 — Next Sentence Prediction (NSP):** A binary classification — given sentence A, the model predicts whether B is the actual next sentence (IsNext) or a random sentence from the corpus (NotNext), with 50/50 sampling. "C is used for next sentence prediction (NSP)." [8]

After pre-training, "the BERT model is first initialized with the pre-trained parameters, and all of the parameters are fine-tuned using labeled data from the downstream tasks" [3] — a two-stage regime the original Transformer does not employ.

## Summary

The original Transformer is a **bidirectional encoder + left-to-right (autoregressive) decoder** trained **supervised for sequence-to-sequence translation**. BERT reuses only the Transformer's **encoder**, drops the autoregressive masking to gain **full bidirectionality**, and replaces direct supervision with **MLM + NSP self-supervised pre-training** followed by task-specific fine-tuning. The encoder-only, fully bidirectional, self-supervised design is what makes BERT suitable as a general-purpose language representation model rather than a translator.

Sources
[1] Attention Is All You Need, Attention Is All You Need, p. 1
[2] Attention Is All You Need, 3 Model Architecture, p. 3
[3] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, 2.2 Unsupervised Fine-tuning Approaches, p. 3
[4] Attention Is All You Need, 3.2.2 Multi-Head Attention, p. 5
[5] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, 1 Introduction, p. 2
[6] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, 5 Ablation Studies, p. 8
[7] Attention Is All You Need, 5.4 Regularization, p. 8
[8] BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding, 3 BERT, p. 4
